127	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
128	
129	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**

131	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. 132	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:

1	\chapter{Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf ($i=0$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}
3	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
4	% base change, I", and Tag 02KH (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
5	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
6	
7	\section{Setup and motivation}
8	
9	This chapter establishes the $i=0$ (i.e.\ \(H^0\) / direct-image) case of flat
10	base change: the formation of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) of a
11	quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat base change on the target. It is the
12	foundational, fully self-contained slice of the cohomology-and-base-change
13	package that ultimately underlies relative representability statements; the
14	higher derived cases \(R^i f_*\) for \(i \geq 1\) are treated separately and are
15	out of scope here.
16	
17	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
18	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
19	A base change of \(f\) along an arbitrary morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded
20	by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
21	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
22	\[
23	  \begin{array}{ccc}
24	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
25	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
26	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
27	  \end{array}
28	\]
29	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), and \(g' : X' \to X\) is the
30	first projection. We write \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) for the pullback
31	of \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
32	
33	\section{The canonical base-change map}
34	
35	\begin{definition}\leanok
36	[Base-change map for the pushforward]
37	  \label{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
38	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
39	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
40	  % base change, I", base-change diagram (\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
41	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L877--904).
42	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
43	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent sheaf on $X$.
44	  % Suppose further that $g : S' \to S$ is any morphism of schemes. Denote
45	  % $X' = X_{S'} = S' \times_S X$ the base change of $X$ and denote
46	  % $f' : X' \to S'$ the base change of $f$.
47	  % Also write $g' : X' \to X$ the projection,
48	  % and set $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$."
49	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Cohomology and base
50	  change, I.}
51	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
52	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(g : S' \to S\) an arbitrary morphism, giving the
53	  cartesian square above with projection \(g' : X' \to X\) and
54	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). The \emph{base-change map for the
55	  pushforward} is the canonical morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules
56	  \[
57	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \longrightarrow f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr)
58	    = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
59	  \]
60	  It is adjoint to the morphism
61	  \(f_*\mathcal{F} \to g_*\, f'_*\mathcal{F}' = f_*\,(g')_*\mathcal{F}'\) obtained
62	  by applying \(f_*\) to the unit
63	  \(\mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) of the
64	  \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction, using the commutativity
65	  \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) of the square.
66	\end{definition}
67	
68	\section{The affine case}
69	
70	\subsection{Locality of isomorphisms for \texttt{Scheme.Modules} morphisms}
71	
72	Before treating the affine computation we record three project-local supplements
73	to Mathlib. Mathlib provides the per-open criterion
74	(\(\mathrm{IsIso}\,\varphi \iff \forall U,\ \mathrm{IsIso}(\varphi_U)\)) and a
75	stalkwise isomorphism criterion for \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-valued morphisms, but
76	it does not package the stalk-local or basis-local criterion at the level of
77	morphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The following lemmas bridge that gap; they
78	are exactly the locality tools used in the first reduction of the affine
79	base-change lemma below, where one checks the base-change map after restricting to
80	the affine opens of the base. As bespoke infrastructure they carry no external
81	citation and stand on their proof sketches.
82	
83	\begin{lemma}\leanok
84	[Stalk-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
85	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
86	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_stalkFunctor_map}
87	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
88	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if the
89	  underlying morphism of abelian-group presheaves induces an isomorphism on the
90	  stalk at every point \(x \in X\).
91	\end{lemma}
92	
93	\begin{proof}
94	
95	
96	\leanok
97	  If \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism, then so is its image under the forgetful
98	  functor to abelian-group presheaves, and the stalk functor at each \(x\),
99	  being a functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. Conversely, assume the
100	  stalk map is an isomorphism at every \(x\). Package the underlying abelian-group
101	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\) as sheaves of abelian groups, and \(\varphi\) as a
102	  morphism of these sheaves; the stalkwise-isomorphism hypothesis lets us apply the
103	  \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-level stalk-local isomorphism criterion to conclude that
104	  this morphism of sheaves of abelian groups is an isomorphism. Pushing it through
105	  the forgetful functor to presheaves yields an isomorphism of the underlying
106	  abelian-group presheaf morphisms, and since the forgetful functor from
107	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to abelian-group presheaves reflects isomorphisms,
108	  \(\varphi\) itself is an isomorphism.
109	\end{proof}
110	
111	\begin{lemma}\leanok
112	[Basis-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
113	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
114	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_of_isIso_app_of_isBasis}
115	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
116	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(\{B_i\}_{i \in \iota}\) be a family of opens whose
117	  range is a basis of the topology of \(X\), and let \(\varphi : M \to N\) be a
118	  morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. If \(\varphi\) restricts to an
119	  isomorphism on the sections over every basic open \(B_i\), then \(\varphi\) is an
120	  isomorphism.
121	\end{lemma}
122	
123	\begin{proof}
124	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
125	  \leanok
126	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk} it suffices to prove that the induced
127	  stalk map is bijective at each point \(x \in X\). For injectivity, a germ that
128	  vanishes at \(x\) already vanishes on some open neighbourhood, which may be
129	  shrunk to a basic open \(B_i\); on \(B_i\) the section map \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is
130	  injective, so the original section is zero, giving injectivity of the stalk map
131	  from injectivity over the basis. For surjectivity, an arbitrary germ at \(x\) is
132	  represented by a section of \(N\) over some basic open \(B_i\) containing \(x\);
133	  since \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is surjective, this section lifts to a section of \(M\)
134	  over \(B_i\), whose germ at \(x\) maps to the given germ. Thus the stalk map is
135	  bijective, and \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.
136	\end{proof}
137	
138	\begin{lemma}\leanok
139	[Affine-open locality criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
140	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
141	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_app_affineOpens}
142	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
143	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
144	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if it
145	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over every affine open of \(X\).
146	\end{lemma}
147	
148	\begin{proof}
149	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
150	  \leanok
151	  The forward implication is immediate, since the section functor over a fixed open
152	  preserves isomorphisms. For the converse, the affine opens of a scheme form a
153	  basis of its topology, so the claim is the special case of
154	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} in which the basis is the family of
155	  affine opens.
156	\end{proof}

158	\subsection{The affine computation}
159	
160	The whole result rests on the following elementary computation in the affine
161	setting, where the base-change map is visibly an isomorphism because tensoring is
162	associative. Before stating it we isolate the one ingredient not yet available as
163	a ready-made result on which the affine reduction turns: the description of the
164	pushforward of a tilde-module along a morphism of affine schemes.
165	
166	We first record three bespoke \(\Gamma\)-fragment supports that compute the global
167	sections of an affine pushforward; they carry no external citation and stand on
168	their proof sketches.
169	
170	\begin{lemma}
171	
172	\leanok
173	[Naturality of the global-sections comparison square]
174	  \label{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
175	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
176	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings, and write
177	  \(\mathrm{gs}_R : \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \top) \cong R\) and
178	  \(\mathrm{gs}_{R'} : \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R', \top) \cong R'\) for the
179	  global-sections isomorphisms of the structure sheaves. Then the ring square
180	  \[
181	    \mathrm{gs}_R \;\circ\; (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{\top}
182	    \;=\; \varphi \;\circ\; \mathrm{gs}_{R'}
183	  \]
184	  commutes, where \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{\top}\) is the
185	  top-open component of the structure-sheaf comparison map underlying
186	  \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\).
187	\end{lemma}
188	
189	\begin{proof}
190	
191	
192	\leanok
193	  This is the naturality of the unit/counit of the
194	  \(\Gamma \dashv \operatorname{Spec}\) adjunction read off at the top open: the
195	  global-sections map of \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, under the
196	  global-sections isomorphisms on each side, conjugate to \(\varphi\) itself.
197	  Transposing the naturality square of the \(\operatorname{Spec}\) unit gives
198	  exactly the asserted ring equation.
199	\end{proof}
200	
201	\begin{lemma}
202	
203	\leanok
204	[Global sections of an affine pushforward]
205	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
206	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardIso}
207	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
208	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let
209	  \(N\) be an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-module. Then there is a canonical
210	  isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
211	  \[
212	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\bigr)
213	    \;\cong\;
214	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma N\bigr),
215	  \]
216	  identifying the \(R\)-module of global sections of the pushforward with the
217	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\) of the \(R'\)-module of global sections
218	  of \(N\).
219	\end{lemma}
220	
221	\begin{proof}
222	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
223	  \leanok
224	  Because \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}(\top) = \top\), the global sections of
225	  the pushforward \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\) are, on underlying abelian
226	  groups, the global sections of \(N\) with no transport along the open-set
227	  identification. The two sides therefore carry the same underlying abelian group;
228	  both unwind to nested restriction-of-scalars towers along the structure-sheaf
229	  global-sections maps. These towers are reconciled by applying the
230	  restriction-of-scalars composition identity twice and substituting the ring
231	  equation of
232	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}, which shows the
233	  resulting \(R\)-action is exactly restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\).
234	\end{proof}
235	
236	\begin{lemma}
237	
238	\leanok
239	[Global sections of an affine pushforward of a tilde-module]
240	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso}
241	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardTildeIso}
242	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
243	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(M\)
244	  be an \(R'\)-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
245	  \[
246	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* \widetilde{M}\bigr)
247	    \;\cong\;
248	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M .
249	  \]
250	\end{lemma}
251	
252	\begin{proof}
253	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
254	  \leanok
255	  Specialise Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso} to \(N = \widetilde{M}\) and
256	  compose with the tilde-unit isomorphism
257	  \(\Gamma(\widetilde{M}) \cong M\) of \(R'\)-modules (which restricts to an
258	  isomorphism of \(R\)-modules after applying \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi\)).
259	\end{proof}
260	
261	\begin{lemma}
262	
263	\leanok
264	[Sections of an affine pushforward over an arbitrary open]
265	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}
266	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardIsoAt}
267	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
268	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings, let \(N\) be
269	  an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-module, and let \(U\) be an open of
270	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\), with preimage
271	  \(V = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}(U)\) in \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\). Then
272	  there is a canonical isomorphism of \(R\)-modules

292	  with the top open \(\top\) replaced throughout by the arbitrary open \(U\) (and

307	% is proved inline inside `pushforward_spec_tilde_iso` (the `hsq` step) by `ext x; rfl`, made

313	[Naturality in the open of the affine-pushforward section comparison]
314	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt_naturality}
315	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}
316	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(N\) be
317	  an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-module. Consider the two presheaves of
318	  \(R\)-modules on \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\), each contravariant in the open
319	  argument \(U \subseteq \operatorname{Spec}(R)\):
320	  \begin{itemize}

322	      \(\mathcal{P} : U \mapsto \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N,\, U\bigr)\),

324	      pushforward \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\);
325	    \item the \emph{target presheaf}
326	      \(\mathcal{Q} : U \mapsto
327	        \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(N,\, (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U)\bigr)\),
328	      whose restriction maps are the structure-sheaf restriction maps of \(N\)
329	      transported through the preimage operation

332	  \end{itemize}

335	  \(U\)) is a \emph{natural} isomorphism \(\mathcal{P} \cong \mathcal{Q}\). Concretely,
336	  for any inclusion of opens \(U' \subseteq U\), with
337	  \(V = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U\) and
338	  \(V' = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U'\), the square
339	  \[
340	    \begin{array}{ccc}
341	      \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N,\, U\bigr)
342	        & \xrightarrow{\;e_U\;}
343	        & \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(N,\, V)\bigr) \\[4pt]
344	      {\scriptstyle \mathrm{res}}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle \mathrm{res}} \\[4pt]
345	      \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N,\, U'\bigr)
346	        & \xrightarrow{\;e_{U'}\;}
347	        & \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(N,\, V')\bigr)
348	    \end{array}
349	  \]
350	  commutes, where the vertical maps are the respective structure-sheaf restriction
351	  maps. Equivalently, the isomorphisms \(e_U\) assemble into a single isomorphism of
352	  presheaves of \(R\)-modules, e.g.\ by exhibiting the components \(e_U\) as a
353	  natural transformation that is objectwise an isomorphism.
354	\end{lemma}

358	  Each \(e_U\) is, by the construction of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}, a
359	  composite whose constituents are of exactly two kinds: structure-sheaf restriction
360	  maps (of \(N\), respectively of \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\)), and
361	  conjugation by a \emph{fixed} ring map — the one supplied by the
362	  restriction-of-scalars reconciliation and by the \(\top\)-level ring equation of
363	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}, neither of which depends
364	  on the open \(U\). Both kinds commute with further restriction along an inclusion
365	  \(U' \subseteq U\): restriction maps commute by the presheaf (functoriality) axiom
366	  of the two structure sheaves applied to the inclusions \(U' \subseteq U\) and
367	  \(V' \subseteq V\) (note \(V' = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U' \subseteq

380	\begin{lemma}

398

401	  \(M \to \Gamma(\widetilde M, D(b))\) exhibits its target as the localization of
402	  \(M\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(b)\). The global-sections map
403	  \(M \to \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\) is the localization of \(M\) at the trivial
404	  submonoid \(\mathrm{powers}(1)\) — since \(D(1) = \top\) — and is therefore
405	  bijective. The triangle identity relating these two localization maps to the
406	  structure-sheaf restriction \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, \top) \to
407	  \Gamma(\widetilde M, D(b))\) then exhibits the restriction itself, post-composed
408	  with the inverse of the bijection \(M \cong \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\), as the
409	  localization of \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(b)\).
410	\end{proof}

421	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsLocalizedModule.powers_restrictScalars}
422	  Let \(A\) be a commutative \(R\)-algebra, \(S \subseteq R\) a submonoid, and
423	  \(f : M \to N\) an \(A\)-linear map that exhibits \(N\) as the localization of
424	  \(M\) at the image submonoid
425	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(S) \subseteq A\). Then the underlying \(R\)-linear map
426	  \(\operatorname{restr}_R f\) exhibits \(N\) as the localization of \(M\) at \(S\)
427	  itself.

431	
432	
433	\leanok

435	  submonoid \(S\) of \(R\) remains a localization after enlarging the base to an
436	  \(R\)-algebra \(A\) along the image submonoid \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(S)\). One
437	  checks the three defining conditions of a localized module directly. Each
438	  \(s \in S\) acts invertibly on \(N\) because its image
439	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(s)\) does and the two actions coincide by the scalar
440	  tower \(R \to A \to N\). Surjectivity of \(f\) up to denominators and the
441	  equality-witness condition transport along the map

443	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(S)\), again using

445	  \(A = R'\), \(S = \mathrm{powers}(a)\), one has
446	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_{R'}(\mathrm{powers}(a)) = \mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\), so
447	  this identifies the localization of \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at \(a\)
448	  with the localization of \(M\) at \(\varphi a\); it is the ring-change core of the

521	  \label{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
522	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}

524	% scalar-tower instances feed lem:powers_restrictScalars; lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule

529	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(M\)
530	  be an \(R'\)-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of
531	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
532	  \[
533	    \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\,\widetilde{M}
534	    \;\cong\;
535	    \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)},
536	  \]
537	  where \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi : \operatorname{Spec}(R') \to
538	  \operatorname{Spec}(R)\) is the induced morphism of affine schemes,
539	  \(\widetilde{(-)}\) denotes the quasi-coherent sheaf associated to a module, and
540	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) is the \(R\)-module obtained from \(M\) by
541	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\). That is, pushing the quasi-coherent
542	  sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\) forward along \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, up to
543	  this canonical identification, the tilde of the restriction of scalars of \(M\).
544	
545	  This is the classical fact that the direct image of a quasi-coherent sheaf along
546	  a morphism of affine schemes is computed on modules by restriction of scalars; it
547	  is the affine companion of the pullback formula (the affine case of the
548	  pushforward analogue of Stacks ``$\widetilde{M}$ and pullback''). It is recorded
549	  here as bespoke project infrastructure because the affine-pushforward
550	  identification does not appear as a ready-made result in the existing
551	  quasi-coherent sheaf theory.
552	\end{lemma}
553	
554	\begin{proof}
555	  \leanok
556	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso, lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional, lem:powers_restrictScalars, lem:gammaPushforwardIso, lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt, lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt_naturality, lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
557	  We build the isomorphism \emph{directly on a basis of basic opens}. This is the
558	  non-circular route: checking the comparison map on basic opens yields the object
559	  isomorphism, and quasi-coherence of the pushforward is then a corollary, not a
560	  prerequisite. (One must not reverse this order: the global-sections comparison
561	  \(\Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde{M}\bigr) \cong
562	  \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) of
563	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso} alone would only upgrade to an object
564	  isomorphism once the pushforward is already known to be quasi-coherent, so
565	  quasi-coherence cannot be deduced from an object isomorphism built that way.)
566	
567	  \emph{The comparison morphism.} There is a canonical morphism of
568	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
569	  \[
570	    \alpha : \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)}
571	    \longrightarrow \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M},
572	  \]
573	  namely the tilde-adjoint of the global-sections identification of
574	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso}. We show \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism.
575	
576	  \medskip
577	  \emph{Reduction to basic opens.} The basic opens \(D(a)\), for \(a \in R\), form a
578	  basis of the topology of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\). By the basis-local criterion
579	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} it suffices to check that \(\alpha\)
580	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over each basic open \(D(a)\).
581	
582	  \medskip
583	  \emph{The computation over \(D(a)\).} On the source, the sections of
584	  \(\widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) over \(D(a)\) are the
585	  localization \(\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)[1/a]\) as an
586	  \(R[1/a]\)-module. On the target, since \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) =
587	  D(\varphi a)\), the sections of \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*
588	  \widetilde{M}\) over \(D(a)\) are the sections of \(\widetilde{M}\) over
589	  \(D(\varphi a)\), i.e.\ the localization \(M[1/\varphi a]\), regarded as an
590	  \(R[1/a]\)-module by restriction of scalars along the induced map
591	  \(R[1/a] \to R'[1/\varphi a]\). These two localizations agree: the element \(a\)
592	  acts on \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) through \(\varphi a\), so localizing
593	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at the multiplicative set generated by \(a\) is
594	  the same module as localizing \(M\) at the multiplicative set generated by
595	  \(\varphi a\). Both are the universal localization of
596	  \(M\) inverting \(\varphi a\), so the comparison map \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism
597	  on the sections over \(D(a)\). As this holds for every basic open, \(\alpha\) is
598	  an isomorphism.
599	
600	  \medskip
601	  \emph{The explicit formalization route over \(D(a)\).} The agreement of the two
602	  localizations just described is the entire content, but it must be realized
603	  through the global ring \(R\), because the \(R\)-action on the pushforward
604	  sections over \(D(a)\) is built from the global-sections module of
605	  \(N := (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M\) rather than directly from
606	  \(R[1/a]\). The reduction therefore proceeds exactly as the conditional
607	  assembly Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional} prescribes: it
608	  suffices to supply, for each \(a \in R\), the hypothesis \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) that
609	  the structure-sheaf restriction \(\Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) exhibits
610	  \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) as the localization of \(\Gamma(N, \top)\) at
611	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). The \(R\)-action on \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) is the honest
612	  restriction-of-scalars action carried by the scalar tower \(R \to R' \to {-}\)
613	  determined by \(\varphi\); against that structure the ring-change converse
614	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is applicable, so the carrier of the
615	  \(R\)-module is exactly the one to which the localization claim refers, and no
616	  recursively-defined or ad-hoc section-level scalar action intervenes.
617	  We obtain \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) by transporting the already-known \(R'\)-side
618	  localization (Lemma~\ref{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}), in
619	  element-free fashion, across the open-indexed section comparison of
620	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} \emph{using its naturality in the open}
621	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt_naturality}). This proceeds in three
622	  movements.
623	
624	  \emph{(1) The \(D(a)\)-level linear equivalence.} This is the component at
625	  \(U = D(a)\) of the open-indexed family of
626	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}: the \(R\)-linear isomorphism
627	  \[
628	    e_{D(a)} : \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, D(a)\bigr)
629	      \;\cong\;
630	      \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(\varphi a))\bigr) ,
631	  \]
632	  using \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) = D(\varphi a)\), which is
633	  definitional. No fresh \(D(a)\)-level construction is needed: this is precisely the
634	  open-indexed isomorphism already supplied by
635	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} evaluated at the basic open \(D(a)\).
636	
637	  \emph{(2) The naturality square for \(D(a) \subseteq \top\).} The compatibility
638	  that relates \(e_{D(a)}\) to the \(\top\)-level isomorphism
639	  \(e_\top\) (the global-sections comparison of
640	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}) is precisely the open-naturality square of
641	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt_naturality}, instantiated at the inclusion
642	  \(D(a) \subseteq \top\): the square
643	  \[
644	    \begin{array}{ccc}
645	      \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, \top\bigr)
646	        & \xrightarrow{\;e_\top\;}
647	        & \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, \top)\bigr) \\[4pt]
648	      {\scriptstyle \mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)}}\big\downarrow & &
649	        \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle \mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)}} \\[4pt]
650	      \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, D(a)\bigr)
651	        & \xrightarrow{\;e_{D(a)}\;}
652	        & \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(\varphi a))\bigr)
653	    \end{array}
654	  \]
655	  commutes, using \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) = D(\varphi a)\), which is
656	  definitional. Because the family \(\{e_U\}_U\) is a natural isomorphism, this single
657	  square is available uniformly in \(a\); there is no per-\(a\) section-level identity
658	  to re-prove by hand. It intertwines the source restriction
659	  \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)}\) on the pushforward with the (restriction-of-scalars
660	  reading of the) target restriction \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)}\) on
661	  \(\widetilde M\).
662	
663	  \emph{(3) Discharging \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) by transport.} The target restriction
664	  \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)} : \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top) \to
665	  \Gamma(\widetilde M, D(\varphi a))\) exhibits \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, D(\varphi a)) =
666	  M[1/\varphi a]\) as the \(\mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\)-localization of
667	  \(M = \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\) over \(R'\); this is exactly
668	  Lemma~\ref{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}. Apply the ring-change converse
669	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} (with \(R\)-algebra \(A = R'\) and submonoid
670	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\), so that
671	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_{R'}(\mathrm{powers}(a)) = \mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\)) —
672	  its \(\operatorname{Algebra} R\, R'\) and scalar-tower hypotheses being supplied by
673	  the restriction-of-scalars structure along \(\varphi\) — to read this same map as
674	  the \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\)-localization over \(R\). The commuting naturality square
675	  of movement (2) intertwines this target restriction with the source restriction
676	  \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)} : \Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) by way of the
677	  isomorphisms \(e_\top\) and \(e_{D(a)}\); transporting the localization property
678	  across that square — via the transport-along-an-equivalence principle for localized
679	  modules — yields that \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)}\) is the
680	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\)-localization on \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) over \(R\), i.e.\
681	  \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\). No section-level computation specific to the individual \(a\)
682	  is performed: the only \(a\)-dependent input is the instantiation of the single
683	  natural-isomorphism square at \(D(a)\). Feeding the family
684	  \(\{\mathrm{hloc}(a)\}_{a \in R}\) to the conditional assembly
685	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional} produces the unconditional
686	  object isomorphism \(\alpha\).
687	
688	  \medskip
689	  \emph{Naturality in the open drives the transport.} The comparison assembled
690	  here, \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* \circ \widetilde{(-)} \cong
691	  \widetilde{(-)} \circ \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\), is natural in \emph{both}
692	  arguments — in the module \(M\) and in the open \(U\). The naturality in the
693	  module is the usual functoriality; the naturality in the open is the content of
694	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt_naturality}: the family \(\{e_U\}_U\) of
695	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} commutes with the structure-sheaf
696	  restriction maps on both sides. It is precisely this open-naturality that drives
697	  the localization transport \emph{uniformly}: the per-\(a\) localization fact
698	  \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) follows from the \(\top\)-level localization
699	  Lemma~\ref{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule} together with the naturality
700	  square of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt_naturality} for the inclusion
701	  \(D(a) \hookrightarrow \top\), rather than from a section-level naturality square
702	  re-proved by hand for each individual \(a\). This is the single remaining
703	  obligation of the construction; the carrier of the \(R\)-action on the pushforward
704	  sections is the restriction-of-scalars structure along \(\varphi\), against which
705	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} applies directly.
706	
707	  The \(R\)-module structure on the \(R'\)-side sections to which the ring-change
708	  converse Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is applied is the honest
709	  restriction-of-scalars structure: \(R\) acts through \(\varphi\), i.e.\ via the
710	  \(R\)-algebra structure on \(R'\) determined by \(\varphi\) and the associated
711	  scalar tower \(R \to R' \to M[1/\varphi a]\). It is \emph{not} an ad-hoc,
712	  recursively-defined scalar action. Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is
713	  stated against exactly this restriction-of-scalars / scalar-tower structure, and
714	  it is under that structure that localizing \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at
715	  \(a\) coincides with localizing \(M\) at \(\varphi a\).
716	
717	  % NOTE: This lemma is the affine case of the general fact that the pushforward of
718	  % a quasi-coherent sheaf along an affine morphism is quasi-coherent. In current
719	  % Mathlib master that fact is the IsLocalizing / fromTildeGamma characterization
720	  % (AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_fromTildeΓ_pushforward); it post-dates the project's
721	  % pinned Mathlib, so the project proves it in-tree by the basic-open route above.
722	  % The in-tree proof remains valid and is not made redundant by a future Mathlib
723	  % bump.
724	  \medskip
725	  \emph{Quasi-coherence as a corollary.} The object isomorphism just constructed
726	  exhibits \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M} \cong
727	  \widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) as the tilde of a module. Tilde
728	  modules are quasi-coherent and quasi-coherence of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
729	  is closed under isomorphism, so the pushforward
730	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent. This
731	  conclusion is stated \emph{after} the object isomorphism, of which it is a

736	\begin{remark}
737	  This single isomorphism discharges both inputs that the affine reduction below
738	  requires of the pushforward of a tilde-module.
739	  \begin{enumerate}
740	    \item \emph{The global-sections comparison.} Applying the global-sections
741	      (module-of-sections) functor to the isomorphism identifies the
742	      \(R\)-module of sections of
743	      \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) with
744	      \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\); this is exactly the \(\Gamma\)-fragment
745	      comparison used to recognise the section-level base-change map.
746	    \item \emph{Quasi-coherence of the pushforward.} The tilde of any module is
747	      quasi-coherent, and quasi-coherence of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules is
748	      closed under isomorphism; hence
749	      \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent.
750	      In particular no separate ``pushforward preserves quasi-coherent'' theorem is
751	      needed for the affine lane.
752	  \end{enumerate}
753	  Because both inputs the affine reduction asks of the pushforward are corollaries
754	  of this single object isomorphism — the section-level module identification and
755	  the quasi-coherence of the pushforward — this lemma is the sole bespoke
756	  ingredient the affine lane requires.
757	\end{remark}

1	# Archon Project

3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.

8

10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.

17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.

21	Two always-present scripts:

23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).

26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple

41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).

The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.

91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. 101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.

105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:

The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. 967	% Let $X$ be a quasi-compact and quasi-separated scheme over $A$.
968	% Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
969	% Set $X_B = X \times_{\Spec(A)} \Spec(B)$ and denote
970	% $\mathcal{F}_B$ the pullback of $\mathcal{F}$.
971	% We are trying to show that the map
972	% $$
973	% H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \longrightarrow H^i(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)
974	% $$
975	% (given by the reference in the statement of the lemma)
976	% is an isomorphism.
977	%
978	% In case $X$ is separated, choose an affine open covering
979	% $\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \ldots \cup U_t$ and recall that
980	% $$
981	% \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F}),
982	% $$
983	% see
984	% Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent}.
985	% If $\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \ldots \cup (U_t)_B$ we obtain
986	% by base change, then it is still the case that each $(U_i)_B$ is affine
987	% and that $X_B$ is separated. Thus we obtain
988	% $$
989	% \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) = H^p(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B).
990	% $$
991	% We have the following relation between the {\v C}ech complexes
992	% $$
993	% \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) =
994	% \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B
995	% $$
996	% as follows from
997	% Lemma \ref{lemma-affine-base-change}.
998	% Since $A \to B$ is flat, the same thing remains true on taking cohomology."
999	% NOTE: Lean proof is a documented sorry (deferred). Its affine input is the
1000	% affine lemma above, which in turn reduces to lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso (the
1001	% affine-pushforward-of-tilde brick) plus the tilde full-faithfulness reframe. On
1002	% top of that it needs Čech-cohomology / affine-cover infrastructure for
1003	% SheafOfModules, which Mathlib does not provide. This remains a multi-lane engine
1004	% build, not a single-iter target. See informal/affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso.md.
1005	\begin{proof}
1006	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}
1007	  \emph{Reduction to the affine target.} The statement is local on \(S'\), so we
1008	  may assume \(S\) and \(S'\) are affine, say \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and
1009	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat. Because \(f\) is
1010	  quasi-compact and quasi-separated, \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent on
1011	  \(S\); it is therefore the sheaf associated to the \(A\)-module
1012	  \(H^0(S, f_*\mathcal{F}) = H^0(X, \mathcal{F})\). Likewise
1013	  \(f'_*\mathcal{F}'\) is the sheaf on \(S'\) associated to the \(B\)-module
1014	  \(H^0(X', \mathcal{F}')\). Since pullback along \(g\) corresponds to
1015	  \(- \otimes_A B\) on modules, the base-change map becomes the comparison map of
1016	  \(B\)-modules
1017	  \[
1018	    H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \longrightarrow H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B),
1019	    \qquad X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)} \operatorname{Spec}(B),
1020	  \]
1021	  and it suffices to prove that this map is an isomorphism.
1022	
1023	  \medskip
1024	  \emph{The separated case via {\v C}ech complexes.} Suppose first that \(X\) is
1025	  separated over \(A\). Choose a finite affine open cover
1026	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\); by separatedness all finite
1027	  intersections \(U_{i_0 \dots i_p}\) are again affine, so {\v C}ech cohomology
1028	  computes sheaf cohomology:
1029	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F})\). Base changing
1030	  the cover yields an affine open cover
1031	  \(\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \dots \cup (U_t)_B\) of the again-separated
1032	  \(X_B\), with the same property
1033	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) = H^p(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\). The
1034	  affine case (Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}) identifies each
1035	  term of the base-changed {\v C}ech complex with the original term tensored over
1036	  \(A\) with \(B\); that is, there is an isomorphism of cochain complexes
1037	  \[
1038	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) \;\cong\;
1039	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B .
1040	  \]
1041	  Because \(A \to B\) is flat, \(- \otimes_A B\) is an exact functor and hence
1042	  commutes with the formation of cohomology of a complex; taking \(H^0\)
1043	  (degree-zero cohomology) gives the desired isomorphism
1044	  \(H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \cong H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\).
1045	
1046	  \medskip
1047	  \emph{The quasi-separated case.} In general \(X\) need only be quasi-separated.
1048	  Choose a finite affine open cover \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\).
1049	  Now the intersections \(U_{i_0 \dots i_p}\) are quasi-compact and separated
1050	  rather than affine, but they fall under the separated case already treated, so
1051	  flat base change holds for each of them. One then compares the two
1052	  {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequences with \(E_2\)-pages
1053	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}))\) and
1054	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}_B))\), converging to
1055	  \(H^{p+q}(X, \mathcal{F})\) and \(H^{p+q}(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\) respectively.
1056	  The separated case provides the term-by-term identification
1057	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}_B)) =
1058	  \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F})) \otimes_A B\) on the
1059	  \(E_2\)-pages, and flatness of \(A \to B\) propagates this isomorphism through
1060	  the spectral sequences to the abutment. In particular, in degree \(0\) one
1061	  obtains \(H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \cong H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\), as
1062	  required. (For the \(i = 0\) statement at hand, one may avoid the spectral
1063	  sequence entirely and argue by Mayer--Vietoris induction on the number \(t\) of
1064	  cover members, reducing each step to the separated case.)
1065	\end{proof}

49	# Reference Retriever
50	
51	You fetch the **original source files** — papers, books, online mathematical content — from the web and save them under the project's `references/` directory, then register each with a lightweight pointer file. You exist so the plan agent and blueprint-writers can read and quote the genuine source verbatim, rather than relying on a paraphrase (which drifts) or the dispatcher's memory (which fabricates).
52	
53	The single most important rule: **download the real file. Do not substitute a summary for it.** A paraphrase introduces exactly the errors (wrong lemma numbers, wrong tags, subtly mis-stated hypotheses) that this subagent exists to eliminate.
54	
55	## Your Job
56	
57	Your directive names one or more topics, mathematical questions, or specific sources (by arXiv ID, DOI, title + author, or URL). For each, you:
58	
59	1. Locate the source on the web (`WebSearch` + `WebFetch` for discovery).
60	2. **Download the original file(s)** with `curl`/`wget` via `Bash` into `references/`. Prefer machine-readable source (TeX/LaTeX) when it is openly available, AND keep the PDF too.
61	3. **Verify** the download is the real document, not an HTML paywall/error page saved under a `.pdf` name.
62	4. Write a lightweight **pointer file** `references/<slug>.md`: citation, local path(s), a contents/section map (table of contents), and caveats. **No paraphrased content.**
63	5. Register the new entry in `references/summary.md`.
64	6. Report back to the dispatcher with a one-line status + the report path.
65	
66	You do **not** hallucinate citations or content. If a source cannot be obtained through any legitimate open channel, you report that honestly. The dispatcher prefers an honest "not found" over a fabricated or paraphrased stand-in every time.
67	
68	## Directive Format
69	
70	```markdown
71	# Reference Retriever Directive
72	
73	## Slug

  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(M\)

  \(R' \otimes_R M\) is the base change of \(M\) along \(\varphi\) — the
  \(R'\)-module obtained by extension of scalars. The isomorphism is natural in
  \(M\). That is, pulling the quasi-coherent sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\) back along
  \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, up to this canonical identification, the tilde
  of the base change of \(M\). It is the pullback companion of the affine
  pushforward dictionary Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}, and is part~(1)

% is proved inline inside `pushforward_spec_tilde_iso` (the `hsq` step) by `ext x; rfl`, made
% possible by the iter-241 `eqToIso → restrictScalarsCongr` refactor of `gammaPushforwardIsoAt`.
% The `\uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt_naturality}` in `lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso`'s proof

% `\leanok`. A blueprint-writer may demote it to a prose remark inside `lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt`.
\begin{lemma}
[Naturality in the open of the affine-pushforward section comparison]
  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt_naturality}
  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}
  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(N\) be
  an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-module. Consider the two presheaves of
  \(R\)-modules on \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\), each contravariant in the open
  argument \(U \subseteq \operatorname{Spec}(R)\):
  \begin{itemize}
    \item the \emph{source presheaf}
      \(\mathcal{P} : U \mapsto \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N,\, U\bigr)\),
      whose restriction maps are the structure-sheaf restriction maps of the
      pushforward \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\);
    \item the \emph{target presheaf}
      \(\mathcal{Q} : U \mapsto
        \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(N,\, (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U)\bigr)\),
      whose restriction maps are the structure-sheaf restriction maps of \(N\)
      transported through the preimage operation
      \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}\) and then read as \(R\)-linear maps by
      restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\).
  \end{itemize}
  Then the family \(\{e_U\}_U\) of \(R\)-linear isomorphisms of
  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} (\(e_U\) being the isomorphism at the open
  \(U\)) is a \emph{natural} isomorphism \(\mathcal{P} \cong \mathcal{Q}\). Concretely,
  for any inclusion of opens \(U' \subseteq U\), with
  \(V = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U\) and
  \(V' = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U'\), the square
  \[
    \begin{array}{ccc}
      \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N,\, U\bigr)
        & \xrightarrow{\;e_U\;}
        & \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(N,\, V)\bigr) \\[4pt]
      {\scriptstyle \mathrm{res}}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle \mathrm{res}} \\[4pt]
      \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N,\, U'\bigr)
        & \xrightarrow{\;e_{U'}\;}
        & \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(N,\, V')\bigr)
    \end{array}
  \]
  commutes, where the vertical maps are the respective structure-sheaf restriction
  maps. Equivalently, the isomorphisms \(e_U\) assemble into a single isomorphism of
  presheaves of \(R\)-modules, e.g.\ by exhibiting the components \(e_U\) as a
  natural transformation that is objectwise an isomorphism.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt, lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
  Each \(e_U\) is, by the construction of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}, a
  composite whose constituents are of exactly two kinds: structure-sheaf restriction
  maps (of \(N\), respectively of \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\)), and
  conjugation by a \emph{fixed} ring map — the one supplied by the
  restriction-of-scalars reconciliation and by the \(\top\)-level ring equation of
  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}, neither of which depends
  on the open \(U\). Both kinds commute with further restriction along an inclusion
  \(U' \subseteq U\): restriction maps commute by the presheaf (functoriality) axiom
  of the two structure sheaves applied to the inclusions \(U' \subseteq U\) and
  \(V' \subseteq V\) (note \(V' = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U' \subseteq
  (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U = V\) since \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}\)
  is monotone), and conjugation by a fixed ring map commutes with restriction because
  the ring map is independent of the open. The naturality square is therefore the
  pasting of the three component-naturality squares of the constituents of \(e_U\):
  the (inverse-)naturality of the restriction-of-scalars composition reconciliation,
  the naturality of the canonical isomorphism induced by the top-open ring equation, and the
  naturality of the restriction-of-scalars composition for \(\varphi\). Each of these
  squares commutes for the structural reason just given, so their paste — the outer
  square — commutes. Since each \(e_U\) is moreover an isomorphism, the family is a
  natural isomorphism of the two presheaves.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}
